expanded_description: Understanding the fundamental difference between strong and weak acids based on their degree of dissociation in aqueous solution, and how this affects their chemical behavior, conductivity, and reactivity.

core_concept.principle: Strong acids completely dissociate into ions in aqueous solution ($\alpha \approx 100\%$), while weak acids only partially dissociate ($\alpha < 100\%$), establishing an equilibrium between ions and undissociated molecules.
core_concept.explanation: The degree of dissociation ($\alpha$) quantifies the fraction of acid molecules that ionize in solution. For strong acids like HCl, essentially every molecule donates its proton to water, producing high $[\text{H}^+]$ and high conductivity. For weak acids like $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$, only a small fraction ionizes, with most molecules remaining intact, resulting in lower $[\text{H}^+]$ and an equilibrium described by the acid dissociation constant $K_a$.

image.alt: Diagram comparing complete dissociation of strong acid vs. partial dissociation of weak acid
image.caption: Strong acids fully dissociate; weak acids establish equilibrium with mostly undissociated molecules

sections.section_title: Degree of Dissociation: The Key Difference
sections.introduction: The fundamental distinction between strong and weak acids lies in how completely they ionize in water. This degree of dissociation determines their acidity, conductivity, reactivity, and how we calculate their properties. Understanding this concept is essential for predicting acid behavior and performing quantitative calculations.

key_concept.definition: The degree of dissociation ($\alpha$) is the fraction of acid molecules that dissociate into ions in solution: $\alpha = \dfrac{\text{moles dissociated}}{\text{initial moles}} \times 100\%$.
key_concept.context: For strong acids, $\alpha \approx 100\%$; for weak acids, $\alpha$ is typically $<5\%$ for dilute solutions. This difference arises from the strength of the H-A bond and the stability of the conjugate base.

principles.statement: Strong acids dissociate completely ($\text{HA} \rightarrow \text{H}^+ + \text{A}^-$); weak acids dissociate partially ($\text{HA} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{A}^-$), establishing equilibrium.
principles.application: This determines whether we use stoichiometry (strong acids) or equilibrium calculations (weak acids) to find $[\text{H}^+]$.
principles.analogy: Like a faucet: strong acid = fully open (all water flows out); weak acid = barely open (only a trickle flows).

principles.statement: Weak acid dissociation is governed by the acid dissociation constant: $K_a = \dfrac{[\text{H}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$, where larger $K_a$ indicates stronger acid.
principles.application: $K_a$ values allow quantitative comparison of weak acid strengths and calculation of $[\text{H}^+]$ for weak acid solutions.
principles.analogy: Like a balance scale: larger $K_a$ means equilibrium favors products (more dissociation).

components.definition: Acid strength is characterized by degree of dissociation ($\alpha$) and acid dissociation constant ($K_a$).
components.equation: For weak acid: $K_a = \dfrac{[\text{H}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$; for dilute solutions: $[\text{H}^+] \approx \sqrt{K_a \cdot C_0}$
components.interpretation: Strong acids: use $[\text{H}^+] = C_{\text{acid}}$; Weak acids: use equilibrium calculations with $K_a$.

image.alt: Equations showing strong acid complete dissociation vs. weak acid equilibrium
image.caption: Strong acids use single arrow; weak acids use equilibrium arrow

worked_examples.title: Calculating Degree of Dissociation
worked_examples.problem: A 0.10 M solution of acetic acid ($\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$) has $[\text{H}^+] = 1.3 \times 10^{-3}$ M. Calculate the degree of dissociation ($\alpha$).

step_by_step.explanation: Multiply by 100% to express as percentage
Only 1.3% of acetic acid molecules are dissociated; 98.7% remain as intact molecules
step_by_step.calculation: This low $\alpha$ confirms acetic acid is a weak acid

worked_examples.concept_applied: Degree of dissociation quantifies the fraction of acid molecules that ionize in solution.

worked_examples.title: Comparing Strong and Weak Acid $[\text{H}^+]$
worked_examples.problem: Compare $[\text{H}^+]$ in 0.10 M HCl (strong acid) vs. 0.10 M $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ ($K_a = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$, weak acid).

step_by_step.explanation: Divide strong acid $[\text{H}^+]$ by weak acid $[\text{H}^+]$
Same concentration, but strong acid has ~77× higher $[\text{H}^+]$ due to complete vs. partial dissociation
step_by_step.calculation: This explains why strong acids are more corrosive and reactive

worked_examples.final_answer: 0.10 M HCl: $[\text{H}^+] = 0.10$ M; 0.10 M $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$: $[\text{H}^+] = 1.3 \times 10^{-3}$ M; strong acid has ~77× higher $[\text{H}^+]$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Degree of dissociation dramatically affects $[\text{H}^+]$ even at equal concentrations.

sections.key_concept: Memorizing the common strong acids and recognizing patterns helps quickly classify acids as strong or weak, which determines the calculation approach.

classifications.value: Only six acids are considered strong in aqueous solution; all others are weak
characteristics: HCl, HBr, HI (hydrohalic acids, except HF)
$\text{HNO}_3$ (nitric acid)
$\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ (sulfuric acid, first proton only)
$\text{HClO}_4$ (perchloric acid)
classifications.behavior: These acids dissociate completely in water; use $[\text{H}^+] = C_{\text{acid}}$ (or $2 \times C_{\text{acid}}$ for $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ first proton)

examples.explanation: $\text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{H}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq)$; 100% dissociation

First proton: $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{H}^+ + \text{HSO}_4^-$ (strong); Second proton: $\text{HSO}_4^- \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{SO}_4^{2-}$ ($K_a = 1.2 \times 10^{-2}$, weak)

classifications.value: Most acids are weak; they partially dissociate and establish equilibrium
characteristics: Organic acids (carboxylic acids: $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$, $\text{HCOOH}$)
Hydrofluoric acid (HF)
Phosphoric acid ($\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$, all three protons weak)
Carbonic acid ($\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$)
Most acids not on the strong acid list
classifications.behavior: Use equilibrium calculations with $K_a$ to find $[\text{H}^+]$; $[\text{H}^+] < C_{\text{acid}}$

examples.explanation: $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-$; $K_a = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$; $\alpha \approx 1.3\%$ at 0.1 M
Only weak hydrohalic acid; $K_a = 6.8 \times 10^{-4}$; stronger than acetic but still weak

classifications.value: Molecular structure influences whether an acid is strong or weak
characteristics: Bond strength: weaker H-A bond = stronger acid
Electronegativity: more electronegative A = stronger acid (for binary acids)
Resonance stabilization: delocalized conjugate base = stronger acid
Inductive effects: electron-withdrawing groups increase acidity
classifications.behavior: These factors explain why some acids are strong and others weak; useful for predicting relative strengths

examples.explanation: H-Cl bond weaker than H-F; Cl⁻ more stable than F⁻ due to size; HCl strong, HF weak
Electron-withdrawing Cl atoms stabilize conjugate base; trichloroacetic acid ($K_a = 0.23$) much stronger than acetic acid ($K_a = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$)

worked_examples.title: Classifying Acids as Strong or Weak
worked_examples.problem: Classify each acid as strong or weak: (a) $\text{HNO}_3$, (b) $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$, (c) $\text{HClO}_4$, (d) $\text{HCOOH}$, (e) HI.

step_by_step.explanation: Not on strong acid list; phosphoric acid is weak (all three protons)
Organic carboxylic acid; not on strong acid list
Hydroiodic acid; on the strong acid list

worked_examples.concept_applied: Only six acids are strong in aqueous solution; all others are weak.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Relative Acid Strength from Structure
worked_examples.problem: Which is the stronger acid: $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ or $\text{CF}_3\text{COOH}$? Explain using structural factors.

step_by_step.explanation: Both are carboxylic acids; $\text{CF}_3\text{COOH}$ has three fluorine atoms vs. three hydrogens in $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$
Fluorine is highly electronegative; $\text{CF}_3$ group withdraws electron density from the carboxyl group
step_by_step.calculation: Electron withdrawal stabilizes the conjugate base ($\text{CF}_3\text{COO}^-$)

step_by_step.explanation: More stable conjugate base = stronger acid (equilibrium favors dissociation)

worked_examples.final_answer: $\text{CF}_3\text{COOH}$ is stronger due to electron-withdrawing fluorine atoms stabilizing the conjugate base
worked_examples.concept_applied: Inductive effects: electron-withdrawing groups increase acid strength by stabilizing the conjugate base.

sections.section_title: Quantifying Weak Acid Dissociation: The $K_a$ Expression
sections.introduction: For weak acids, the extent of dissociation is quantified by the acid dissociation constant ($K_a$). This equilibrium constant allows us to calculate $[\text{H}^+]$, pH, and degree of dissociation for any weak acid solution.

key_concept.definition: The acid dissociation constant is defined as $K_a = \dfrac{[\text{H}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$ for the equilibrium $\text{HA} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{A}^-$, where concentrations are at equilibrium.
key_concept.context: Larger $K_a$ values indicate stronger weak acids (more dissociation). $K_a$ is temperature-dependent and specific to each acid.

categories.description: ICE (Initial, Change, Equilibrium) tables organize weak acid dissociation calculations
categories.detailed_mechanism: For weak acid HA with initial concentration $C_0$: Initial: $[\text{HA}] = C_0$, $[\text{H}^+] = [\text{A}^-] = 0$; Change: $-x$, $+x$, $+x$; Equilibrium: $C_0 - x$, $x$, $x$

examples.explanation: ICE table: Initial: 0.10, 0, 0; Change: $-x$, $+x$, $+x$; Equilibrium: $0.10-x$, $x$, $x$

categories.description: For weak acids with small $K_a$ and reasonable concentration, $x \ll C_0$, so $C_0 - x \approx C_0$
categories.detailed_mechanism: This simplifies $K_a = \dfrac{x^2}{C_0 - x}$ to $K_a \approx \dfrac{x^2}{C_0}$, giving $x = [\text{H}^+] \approx \sqrt{K_a \cdot C_0}$

examples.explanation: $[\text{H}^+] \approx \sqrt{(1.8 \times 10^{-5})(0.10)} = 1.3 \times 10^{-3}$ M; Check: $x/C_0 = 1.3\% < 5\%$, approximation valid

categories.description: If $x > 5\%$ of $C_0$, the approximation is invalid; solve the quadratic equation
categories.detailed_mechanism: Use $K_a = \dfrac{x^2}{C_0 - x}$ and solve $x^2 + K_a x - K_a C_0 = 0$ using quadratic formula

examples.explanation: Approximation gives $x = 1.3 \times 10^{-4}$ M; $x/C_0 = 13\% > 5\%$; must solve quadratic for accuracy

worked_examples.title: Calculating pH of a Weak Acid Solution
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the pH of 0.10 M acetic acid ($\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$, $K_a = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Weak acid pH calculations use $K_a$ and equilibrium; small $x$ approximation simplifies calculation when valid.

worked_examples.title: Calculating $K_a$ from Measured pH
worked_examples.problem: A 0.050 M solution of a weak acid HA has pH = 3.20. Calculate $K_a$ for HA.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Experimental pH measurements allow determination of $K_a$ for weak acids.

sections.section_title: Consequences of Acid Strength: Conductivity and Reactivity
sections.key_concept: The degree of dissociation affects observable properties of acid solutions, including electrical conductivity, reaction rates with metals, and neutralization behavior.

classifications.value: Conductivity depends on ion concentration; strong acids conduct better than weak acids at equal concentration
characteristics: Strong acids: high $[\text{H}^+]$ and $[\text{A}^-]$ → high conductivity
Weak acids: low ion concentration → low conductivity
Conductivity proportional to total ion concentration
classifications.behavior: Conductivity measurements can distinguish strong vs. weak acids experimentally

examples.explanation: HCl: $[\text{ions}] = 0.2$ M; $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$: $[\text{ions}] \approx 0.0026$ M; HCl conducts ~77× better

classifications.value: Reaction rate with active metals (e.g., Zn, Mg) depends on $[\text{H}^+]$; strong acids react faster
characteristics: Rate $\propto [\text{H}^+]$ for many metal-acid reactions
Strong acids provide higher $[\text{H}^+]$ → faster $\text{H}_2$ production
Same total acid moles, different rates
classifications.behavior: Equal concentrations of strong and weak acids produce $\text{H}_2$ at different initial rates, but same total $\text{H}_2$ if acid is limiting

examples.explanation: 0.1 M HCl produces $\text{H}_2$ bubbles rapidly; 0.1 M $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ produces bubbles slowly; both produce same total $\text{H}_2$ if Zn is excess

classifications.value: Total moles of base needed for neutralization depends on total acid moles, not strength
characteristics: Strong and weak acids of equal concentration and volume require equal moles of base
Weak acids may neutralize more slowly due to equilibrium
Endpoint pH differs: strong acid-strong base = pH 7; weak acid-strong base = pH > 7
classifications.behavior: Stoichiometry is the same; kinetics and endpoint pH differ

examples.process: Titrating 0.1 M HCl vs. 0.1 M $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ with NaOH
examples.explanation: Both require same volume of NaOH for equivalence; but $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ titration has equivalence point at pH ~8.7, not 7.0

worked_examples.title: Predicting Conductivity from Acid Strength
worked_examples.problem: Which solution conducts electricity better: 0.01 M $\text{HNO}_3$ or 0.01 M $\text{HCOOH}$ ($K_a = 1.8 \times 10^{-4}$)? Explain.

step_by_step.explanation: Strong acid: $[\text{ions}] = 2 \times 0.01 = 0.02$ M; Weak acid: $[\text{H}^+] \approx \sqrt{K_a \cdot C_0} = \sqrt{(1.8 \times 10^{-4})(0.01)} = 1.3 \times 10^{-3}$ M; $[\text{ions}] \approx 2.6 \times 10^{-3}$ M
Conductivity proportional to ion concentration; more ions = better conduction
Strong acid has higher ion concentration due to complete dissociation

worked_examples.final_answer: 0.01 M $\text{HNO}_3$ conducts better because it is a strong acid with ~7.7× higher ion concentration than the weak acid $\text{HCOOH}$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Degree of dissociation determines ion concentration, which determines electrical conductivity.

worked_examples.title: Neutralization Stoichiometry vs. Kinetics
worked_examples.problem: Equal volumes of 0.10 M HCl and 0.10 M $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ are each titrated with 0.10 M NaOH. Compare: (a) volume of NaOH to reach equivalence, (b) initial reaction rate, (c) pH at equivalence.

step_by_step.explanation: Stoichiometry: 1 mol acid reacts with 1 mol NaOH for both; equal moles of acid in equal volumes
step_by_step.calculation: Same volume of NaOH required for both

step_by_step.explanation: Rate depends on $[\text{H}^+]$; HCl has $[\text{H}^+] = 0.10$ M; $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ has $[\text{H}^+] \approx 0.0013$ M
HCl + NaOH → NaCl + $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (neutral salt, pH = 7); $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ + NaOH → $\text{CH}_3\text{COONa}$ + $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (basic salt, $\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-$ hydrolyzes)

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Same NaOH volume; (b) HCl reacts faster initially; (c) HCl equivalence pH = 7.0, $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ equivalence pH ≈ 8.7
worked_examples.concept_applied: Acid strength affects kinetics and endpoint pH, but not neutralization stoichiometry.

sections.introduction: Some acids can donate more than one proton. These polyprotic acids dissociate in sequential steps, each with its own $K_a$ value. Understanding this stepwise dissociation is crucial for accurate pH calculations and predicting buffer behavior.

key_concept.definition: Polyprotic acids dissociate in multiple steps: $\text{H}_n\text{A} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{H}_{n-1}\text{A}^-$ ($K_{a1}$), $\text{H}_{n-1}\text{A}^- \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{H}_{n-2}\text{A}^{2-}$ ($K_{a2}$), etc., with $K_{a1} > K_{a2} > K_{a3}$.
key_concept.context: Each successive proton is harder to remove because the conjugate base becomes more negatively charged, making $K_a$ values decrease by factors of $10^3$-$10^6$ per step.

categories.description: Acids that donate two protons, with two $K_a$ values
categories.detailed_mechanism: First dissociation usually dominates $[\text{H}^+]$; second dissociation contributes minimally unless $K_{a1}$ and $K_{a2}$ are close

examples.explanation: Step 1: $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{H}^+ + \text{HSO}_4^-$ (strong, $K_{a1} \gg 1$); Step 2: $\text{HSO}_4^- \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{SO}_4^{2-}$ ($K_{a2} = 1.2 \times 10^{-2}$, weak)
Step 1: $K_{a1} = 4.3 \times 10^{-7}$; Step 2: $K_{a2} = 5.6 \times 10^{-11}$; $K_{a1} \gg K_{a2}$, so first dissociation dominates

categories.description: Acids that donate three protons, with three $K_a$ values
categories.detailed_mechanism: Each step progressively weaker; usually only first dissociation significantly contributes to $[\text{H}^+]$ in dilute solutions

examples.explanation: $K_{a1} = 7.5 \times 10^{-3}$; $K_{a2} = 6.2 \times 10^{-8}$; $K_{a3} = 4.8 \times 10^{-13}$; each step ~$10^5$ weaker than previous

categories.description: For most polyprotic acids, treat as monoprotic using $K_{a1}$ only
categories.detailed_mechanism: If $K_{a1} \gg K_{a2}$ (by factor >$10^3$), second dissociation contributes negligibly to $[\text{H}^+]$; use monoprotic weak acid calculation with $K_{a1}$

examples.explanation: Use $K_{a1} = 4.3 \times 10^{-7}$; ignore $K_{a2}$; $[\text{H}^+] \approx \sqrt{K_{a1} \cdot C_0} = 2.1 \times 10^{-4}$ M; pH ≈ 3.68

worked_examples.title: pH of a Diprotic Acid Solution
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the pH of 0.050 M carbonic acid ($\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$), given $K_{a1} = 4.3 \times 10^{-7}$ and $K_{a2} = 5.6 \times 10^{-11}$.

step_by_step.step: Assess whether to consider both dissociations
step_by_step.calculation: First dissociation dominates; ignore second for $[\text{H}^+]$ calculation

step_by_step.step: Treat as monoprotic weak acid with $K_{a1}$

worked_examples.concept_applied: For polyprotic acids with $K_{a1} \gg K_{a2}$, treat as monoprotic using $K_{a1}$ only.

sections.key_concept: Misunderstanding the distinction between strong and weak acids leads to calculation errors. Systematic approaches ensure correct treatment of each acid type.

classifications.type: Error: Treating Weak Acid as Strong
characteristics: Overestimates $[\text{H}^+]$ by factors of 10-1000
Gives pH too low
Common when $K_a$ not provided or forgotten
classifications.behavior: Always check if acid is on strong acid list; if not, use $K_a$ and equilibrium calculations

examples.explanation: Wrong: $[\text{H}^+] = 0.1$ M, pH = 1.0; Correct: $[\text{H}^+] = 1.3 \times 10^{-3}$ M, pH = 2.89

classifications.type: Error: Using Equilibrium for Strong Acids
classifications.value: Setting up ICE table and $K_a$ expression for strong acids
characteristics: Unnecessary complexity
May lead to incorrect assumptions
Strong acids have no meaningful $K_a$ (very large)
classifications.behavior: Strong acids: use stoichiometry only; $[\text{H}^+] = C_{\text{acid}}$ (or $2 \times C_{\text{acid}}$ for first proton of $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$)

examples.explanation: Wrong: setting up $K_a = \dfrac{x^2}{0.1-x}$; Correct: $[\text{H}^+] = 0.1$ M directly

characteristics: Approximation fails when $x > 5\%$ of $C_0$
Gives significant error for dilute solutions or relatively strong weak acids
Must verify after calculation
classifications.behavior: Always check: if $x/C_0 > 0.05$, solve quadratic equation $x^2 + K_a x - K_a C_0 = 0$

examples.explanation: Approximation gives $x = 1.3 \times 10^{-4}$; $x/C_0 = 13\% > 5\%$; must solve quadratic for accuracy

classifications.value: Assuming larger $C_{\text{acid}}$ always means lower pH, ignoring $K_a$
characteristics: Weak acid at high concentration may have higher pH than strong acid at low concentration
Both $C_{\text{acid}}$ and $K_a$ determine $[\text{H}^+]$
classifications.behavior: Calculate $[\text{H}^+]$ properly for each acid before comparing pH

examples.explanation: Acetic: $[\text{H}^+] \approx \sqrt{(1.8 \times 10^{-5})(1.0)} = 4.2 \times 10^{-3}$ M, pH = 2.38; HCl: $[\text{H}^+] = 0.001$ M, pH = 3.00; weak acid at high concentration can be more acidic than strong acid at low concentration

worked_examples.title: Error Analysis: Weak Acid Treated as Strong
worked_examples.problem: A student calculates pH of 0.050 M HF ($K_a = 6.8 \times 10^{-4}$) as 1.30 by assuming complete dissociation. Identify and correct the error.

step_by_step.explanation: Student treated HF as strong acid; HF is actually weak ($K_a = 6.8 \times 10^{-4}$)
HF is weak; use $K_a$ expression with ICE table

worked_examples.final_answer: Error: treated weak acid HF as strong; correct pH = 2.26 (not 1.30)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Weak acids require equilibrium calculations; never assume complete dissociation unless acid is on strong acid list.

worked_examples.title: Strong vs. Weak Acid Problem-Solving Checklist
worked_examples.problem: Outline a systematic approach for acid strength problems.

step_by_step.explanation: Is the acid on the strong acid list (HCl, HBr, HI, $\text{HNO}_3$, $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, $\text{HClO}_4$)?
step_by_step.calculation: If yes: strong; if no: weak (use $K_a$)

step_by_step.explanation: Use stoichiometry: $[\text{H}^+] = C_{\text{acid}}$ (or $2 \times C_{\text{acid}}$ for first proton of $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$)
Write dissociation equation and $K_a$ expression; set up ICE table

step_by_step.step: Step 4: Apply small $x$ approximation if valid
step_by_step.calculation: Check: if $x/C_0 < 0.05$, approximation valid; if not, solve quadratic

step_by_step.explanation: $[\text{H}^+] = x$; pH = -log$[\text{H}^+]$; apply sig fig rules
step_by_step.calculation: Decimal places in pH = sig figs in $[\text{H}^+]$

worked_examples.final_answer: Follow 5-step checklist: identify acid type, apply appropriate calculation method, verify approximation, calculate pH with correct sig figs
worked_examples.concept_applied: Systematic approach prevents misclassification and calculation errors for strong vs. weak acids.

sections.section_title: Practice Problems: Strong vs. Weak Acids
sections.introduction: Test your understanding with these practice problems. Try solving before looking at solutions.

key_concept.definition: Practice problems reinforce classification of acids, degree of dissociation calculations, and pH calculations for strong and weak acids.
key_concept.context: Cover solutions and attempt each problem independently, then check your work.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 1
worked_examples.problem: Classify each acid as strong or weak: (a) $\text{HClO}_4$, (b) $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_3$, (c) HBr, (d) $\text{HNO}_2$, (e) $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$.

step_by_step.explanation: (a) $\text{HClO}_4$: strong; (b) $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_3$: weak (sulfurous, not sulfuric); (c) HBr: strong; (d) $\text{HNO}_2$: weak (nitrous, not nitric); (e) $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$: strong (first proton)

worked_examples.concept_applied: Only six acids are strong in aqueous solution; similar names can be misleading.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 2
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the pH of 0.025 M HCl.

step_by_step.explanation: pH = -log$[\text{H}^+]$; 2 sig figs in concentration → 2 decimal places in pH

worked_examples.concept_applied: Strong acids: $[\text{H}^+] = C_{\text{acid}}$; direct pH calculation.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 3

step_by_step.explanation: 2 sig figs in $[\text{H}^+]$ → 2 decimal places in pH

worked_examples.concept_applied: Weak acids: use $K_a$ and equilibrium; small $x$ approximation when valid.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 4
worked_examples.problem: A 0.10 M solution of weak acid HA has pH = 2.90. Calculate $K_a$ and degree of dissociation ($\alpha$).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Experimental pH allows determination of $K_a$ and $\alpha$ for weak acids.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 5
worked_examples.problem: Which has lower pH: 0.01 M $\text{HNO}_3$ or 0.10 M $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ ($K_a = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$)? Calculate both pH values.
worked_examples.final_answer: 0.01 M $\text{HNO}_3$ has lower pH (2.00) than 0.10 M $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ (2.89)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Strong acid at lower concentration can be more acidic than weak acid at higher concentration.

summary: Strong acids completely dissociate in aqueous solution ($\alpha \approx 100\%$), while weak acids partially dissociate ($\alpha < 100\%$), establishing equilibrium $\text{HA} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{A}^-$. Only six acids are strong in water: HCl, HBr, HI, $\text{HNO}_3$, $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ (first proton), $\text{HClO}_4$; all others are weak. The degree of dissociation $\alpha = [\text{H}^+]/C_0$ quantifies ionization extent. For weak acids, the acid dissociation constant $K_a = \dfrac{[\text{H}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$ governs equilibrium; larger $K_a$ indicates stronger acid. pH calculations: strong acids use $[\text{H}^+] = C_{\text{acid}}$; weak acids use $[\text{H}^+] \approx \sqrt{K_a \cdot C_0}$ (small $x$ approximation, valid when $\alpha < 5\%$). Polyprotic acids dissociate in steps with $K_{a1} > K_{a2} > K_{a3}$; usually only first dissociation significantly contributes to $[\text{H}^+]$. Consequences of acid strength: strong acids have higher conductivity, faster reaction rates with metals, and neutralization endpoint at pH 7 (vs. pH > 7 for weak acid-strong base). Common errors: treating weak acids as strong, misapplying small $x$ approximation, confusing $K_a$ with concentration. A systematic 5-step approach ensures accuracy: identify acid type, apply appropriate calculation method, verify approximation validity, calculate pH, apply significant figure rules. Mastery of strong vs. weak acid concepts is essential for pH calculations, titration analysis, buffer design, and predicting acid behavior in chemical and biological systems.
